content.problemStatement:
Please note that all arrays in this problem are 0-indexed.

Chef, who is now an elite spy, is in trouble! He needs to quickly send a message to you, who is in his Headquarters. Chef's message is a sequence of NNN non-negative integers.

To avoid the risk of the message being intercepted by the enemy, you and Chef have decided on an elaborate plan. First, Chef will construct his message, which can be represented as the array A0A_0A0​: A0,0,A0,1,A0,2,…,A0,N−1A_{0, 0}, A_{0,1}, A_{0,2}, \dots, A_{0,N-1}A0,0​,A0,1​,A0,2​,…,A0,N−1​. Then, he will construct 2N−12^{N}-12N−1 fake messages, each of which is also a sequence of NNN non-negative integers. The fake messages are numbered from 111 to 2N−12^{N}-12N−1, with the iii-th fake message represented by the array AiA_iAi​. The iii-th fake message is constructed via the following process:


Let the binary representation of iii be denoted as BiB_iBi​.
If the jthj^{th}jth bit of BiB_iBi​ is '0', Ai,j=A0,jA_{i,j} = A_{0,j}Ai,j​=A0,j​.
Otherwise, Ai,jA_{i,j}Ai,j​ can be any non-negative integer except A0,jA_{0,j}A0,j​.
In other words, if Bi,j=0B_{i,j}=0Bi,j​=0, then Ai,j=A0,jA_{i,j} = A_{0,j}Ai,j​=A0,j​, and if Bi,j=1B_{i,j}=1Bi,j​=1, then Ai,j≠A0,jA_{i,j} \neq A_{0,j}Ai,j​=A0,j​.
After the real message and the 2N−12^{N} - 12N−1 fake messages (for a total of M=2NM=2^NM=2N) are constructed, they are all put together and randomly shuffled, so that it is unknown which was the real message.

Let us illustrate this process with an example. Say that N=2N=2N=2. We may randomly construct the real message to be A0=[0,2]A_0 = [0,2]A0​=[0,2]. Now:


B1=[0,1]B_1=[0,1]B1​=[0,1]. It is possible to create A1=[0,1]A_1 = [0,1]A1​=[0,1]. Since B1,0=0B_{1,0}=0B1,0​=0, A1,0=A0,0A_{1,0} = A_{0,0}A1,0​=A0,0​. Since B1,1=1B_{1,1}=1B1,1​=1, A1,1≠A0,1A_{1,1} \neq A_{0,1}A1,1​=A0,1​.
B2=[1,0]B_2=[1,0]B2​=[1,0]. It is possible to create A2=[2,2]A_2=[2,2]A2​=[2,2]. Since B2,0=1B_{2,0}=1B2,0​=1, A2,0≠A0,0A_{2,0} \neq A_{0,0}A2,0​=A0,0​. Since B2,1=0B_{2,1}=0B2,1​=0, A2,1=A0,1A_{2,1} = A_{0,1}A2,1​=A0,1​.
B3=[1,1]B_3=[1,1]B3​=[1,1]. It is possible to create A3=[1,1]A_3=[1,1]A3​=[1,1]. Since B3,0=1B_{3,0}=1B3,0​=1, A3,0≠A0,0A_{3,0} \neq A_{0,0}A3,0​=A0,0​. Since B3,1=1B_{3,1}=1B3,1​=1, A3,1≠A0,1A_{3,1} \neq A_{0,1}A3,1​=A0,1​.
In the end, we have 444 messages: A0=[0,2]A_0=[0,2]A0​=[0,2], A1=[0,1]A_1=[0,1]A1​=[0,1], A2=[2,2]A_2=[2,2]A2​=[2,2], A3=[1,1]A_3=[1,1]A3​=[1,1]. Now, we can randomly shuffle them, and get the following: A0=[1,1]A_0=[1,1]A0​=[1,1], A1=[0,1]A_1=[0,1]A1​=[0,1], A2=[0,2]A_2=[0,2]A2​=[0,2], A3=[2,2]A_3=[2,2]A3​=[2,2]. As we can see, the original real message is now the message A2A_2A2​.

Your task is this: given the final randomly shuffled sequence of messages, determine which message(s) could have been the real message. In particular, in some cases, it may be possible that **more than one** of the messages could have been the real message. (For example, in the preceding example, apart from message 222, message 111 also could have been the real message.) It is also possible that Chef made a mistake when generating the messages and so **none** of the messages could have been the real message. In this case, report this.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
The first line of each testcase contains two integers: NNN and MMM. NNN is the length of each message, and MMM is the number of messages (so M=2NM=2^NM=2N).
Then, MMM lines follow. Each line consists of NNN space-separated non-negative integers and represents one of the messages. In other words, the jjj-th integer on the iii-th of these lines is Ai−1,j−1A_{i-1,j-1}Ai−1,j−1​.


Output Format

For each testcase, print 222 lines of output.
The first line consists of a single integer, KKK. KKK is the number of messages which could have been the real message. Please note that it is possible that K=0K=0K=0 if none of the messages could have been the real message.
The second line consists of KKK space-separated integers - the indices of the messages which could have been the real message, **in ascending order**.
If K=0K=0K=0, the second line of output for the testcase should still be there, but it should be empty.
constraints: The input is divided into multiple subtasks. You will get the points allocated for a subtask if and only if you solve every testcase in the subtask correctly, within the time limit.

Please remember that when you make a submission to a problem, we will automatically consider your submission for every single subtask, and also the full problem. After the contest, your scores will be 'stitched'. This means that if you solve a subtask in any of your submissions, you will get the points for the subtask in your final score. There is no need to combine your solutions for different subtasks into the same submission.
The sum of MMM across all testcases is ≤131072\leq 131 072≤131072.

Please note that when KKK is mentioned, it refers to the KKK in the output. For example, say that K=1K=1K=1 is guaranteed for a subtask. It means that for each testcase, exactly one message could have been the real message.
These are the subtasks for the problem:
Subtask 4 [10 points]: 0≤Ai,j≤10 \leq A_{i,j} \leq 10≤Ai,j​≤1 for all (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), K≥1K \geq 1K≥1

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The first testcase is the same as the example in the problem statement. As discussed, both messages 111 and 222 could have been the real message.
In the second testcase, Chef made a mistake, and none of the messages could have been the real message. Note that there were still two lines of output, but the second line was empty.
In the third testcase, none of the messages could have been the real message.
In the final testcase, all of the messages could have been the real message.

solution.approach: The goal is to identify which of the shuffled messages could have been the real one. Start by analyzing the frequencies of each integer at every position across all messages. The real message must have integers at positions where the majority of messages ( specifically M/2 ) agree, as fake messages only alter values based on the binary representation of their index. For each position in the messages, determine the integer that appears M/2 times (if any) and consider it as a possible value for the corresponding position in the real message. Then, verify the integrity of the identified message by checking whether all fake messages satisfy the rules of generation from this candidate. If any violations occur, the candidate is invalid, and no real message exists. Finally, if a valid real message is found, identify all messages that match the candidate and output their indices.
